data.experience: I do not remember the exact date I was sent there; I was inside for about a month. There was no electric shock or anything extremely out of bounds, but it was fully militarized management with corporal punishment, and if you resisted the instructors you would be beaten. Most of the people there were so-called troublemakers, people who had gotten into trouble outside, or people labeled as internet-addicted. If someone was being "corrected," they would definitely take them. I only stayed for a month, so I cannot confirm every detail, but inside you had to obey the instructors and endure it if you wanted to get out faster. They made you write letters to your parents and record videos, and you had to pretend you understood that you were "wrong." That made it a little easier to survive.

data.experience:
I was asleep in the car that day and had no idea I was being taken to a "correction" institution. Four instructors dragged me out of the vehicle; I struggled, dropped my phone, and the director followed behind picking up my phone and backpack while they searched through my things and even took my snacks. When I reached the so-called little black room, those four instructors threw me straight onto the ground and I cried loudly.

I was assigned to Class 14 of the Third Squadron, whose homeroom teacher was Li Kun. The school had both psychologists and instructors. The instructors watched us every day, while the psychologists occasionally came to do counseling; each psychologist handled about fifteen students. If a dorm was too noisy during shower time, they would call an emergency assembly. Even in winter, they could make you come out half-naked, and if you took more than six seconds they made you do squats. The First and Second Squadrons were even harsher than mine and often used corporal punishment such as squats, 180-degree jumps, front-and-back jumps, and forced running. All four squadrons had similar practices. Runs usually started at one kilometer, and some teachers forced you to keep running if you failed to finish within a set time. One of the things I remember most is people being made to run a total of ten kilometers over six or seven days; even if your training performance was poor, punishment continued until your legs gave out.

The psychological staff were even more outrageous. Some students wanted to write letters home telling their parents what was really happening, but once the psychologists saw the letters they said they were "non-compliant" and refused to send them. Counseling was supposedly there to calm emotions, but it was also used for brainwashing. My assigned psychologist was the chief psychologist. I saw files that stayed open for months even after people had supposedly graduated; they said there was a high chance the monitoring continued into adulthood. If you did not obey and your parents complained to the psychologist, the psychologist might recommend sending you back a second time.

One thing that stuck with me was a classmate who got reported for hitting someone. When the teacher asked him about it, he first denied it, and the teacher slammed him into the wall so hard we could hear the banging. Near the time I left, I was once punished for disobeying. A teacher made me squat off to one side, then decided he did not like how I was doing it and hit me twice with a clothes hanger, hard enough that the marks were still visible days after I got home. He later applied medicated oil, but when I reported him the director simply covered for him.

I also heard older students say that three people once escaped by climbing over the wall, and that two were caught by the dean of discipline and beaten until they vomited blood, though I cannot verify that myself. I personally went through two major crackdowns. On August 31, after a director named Li Dedie was criticized by higher-ups during an inspection, he lost his temper. My squadron had to run fifteen laps, other squadrons did squats, and that night we had to record staged "virtue and self-cultivation" material. Later, because a few people were noisy, everyone was driven downstairs, made to run roughly four kilometers, and then forced to do squats for twenty minutes regardless of count. Even after it started raining, they still kept us standing there for about five minutes before letting us go back. The following Saturday the corporal punishment continued.

The second crackdown was on September 21, and again the punishment went on without stopping even though I was already dizzy. Teachers also threatened people. One summer Monday, when we were supposed to prepare speeches under the national flag and the auditorium was too noisy, a teacher threatened to turn the air conditioner to 30 degrees Celsius and make everyone do squats inside like "roasting rats." Students who stayed there for a full year reportedly went through this kind of mass punishment four times: once in the cafeteria, once in the dorms, and twice in the auditorium. A teacher even bragged about it. During two assessments our class ranked near the bottom, and on October 1, while others were doing activities, we were still training under the blazing sun. It was miserable.

After arriving at the school, the violence got even worse: they forcibly dragged me inside, and the wounds on my legs still had not healed and were covered in blood. data.experience:
On the afternoon of the 30th, my parents, acting on instructions, tricked me into a vehicle disguised as a ride-hailing car for a "correction" institution, where I was forcibly abducted and illegally detained.

When the car left the expressway, I tried to jump out but failed. The driver of the car behind noticed what was happening and called the police, but after the local police learned about it they deliberately did nothing and never contacted me.

That same night my long hair was shaved off.

Daily life mostly involved corporal punishment, brainwashing through so-called "traditional studies," and occasional "psychological counseling." Sleep deprivation and forced drills were common, and hygiene conditions were extremely poor.

I was eventually rescued by friends because I had sent a scheduled message in advance.
data.scandal: Illegal detention and corporal punishment.

data.addr: Henan Province ZhengZhou City DengFeng City DengFengAvenue288 No.
data.experience:
Daily life hardly changed. Reveille was around 6 a.m., followed by a 5 to 8 kilometer run. Most coaches were very young, usually between 17 and 24. Some had no sympathy at all for students who could not keep up and imposed corporal punishment afterward. After the run we went back to the dorm to tidy up and wash, then had breakfast, which was usually mantou, porridge, pickles, and leftovers from the day before. The schedule was generally half a day of martial arts classes and half a day of academic classes, with breaks every one or two hours, though the overall intensity was still high.

Academic teaching quality was generally poor. If you were caught slacking off, you could be punished on the spot. If you were officially criticized, there was a good chance a coach would later beat you in the dormitory with one of three different sticks. Students were forbidden from moving around alone; even free movement had to be done in groups of two or three. If you were caught trying to run away, several coaches and admissions staff could drag you into a room and beat you, sometimes severely enough to leave lasting injuries.

The school had serious clique problems and severe bullying. Some coaches held positions in the group's administrative office or inspection division, so the internal reporting system was ineffective and could even lead to retaliation. Coaches often turned a blind eye unless something became too serious. I was once nearly suffocated with a pillow, and the matter was quietly dropped. Students who were easygoing were routinely bullied, and you could not casually choose sides.

I was also privately questioned by admissions staff after same-sex romantic messages were found on my phone. They lectured me from a traditionalist perspective but did not really intervene with the coaches. Food was not especially bad by institutional standards, and there were sometimes films or rest time on weekends. Even so, this was still a coercive camp. Even if it now tries to present itself as "free of corporal punishment," the underlying confinement and ideological control still remain.
data.scandal: Corporal punishment, concealing students' health conditions, shielding staff, and intentionally causing disabling injury.

data.scandal:
Xiangyang School in Nanchuan District, Chongqing, although designated as a "special school" under the Law on the Prevention of Juvenile Delinquency, is accused of abusing public authority and illegally recruiting students. Despite being a public-interest institution, it allegedly charges parents of non-court-ordered students 2,500 yuan per month, with a six-month minimum payment, and profits by obscuring the school's special legal status while misleading the public into thinking any child can be sent there with parental consent.

It is further accused of taking people into the school against their will and without their prior knowledge, immediately subjecting them to correctional education without any real review or appeal process, and illegally confining them while depriving them of personal freedom and communication with the outside world.

The Nanchuan District Education Commission's stability office is also accused of falsely claiming that the student met the statutory threshold of "serious misconduct," unlawfully approving placement in the special school, and using distorted legal interpretations to expand the so-called family-sent student system.

data.else: This is a larger group with branch campuses in Nanning, Guigang, Guangdong, Beijing, and elsewhere.
data.experience:
I may have written too much, so please forgive me. During what they called the pickup stage, they usually sent one instructor plus two or three long-term residents to seize someone at the address. After grabbing the person, they would often buy them one meal and one drink to take to the camp as a so-called last meal. Escape was basically impossible: the camp was in a newly developed area, confinement was extremely strict, the surroundings were sparse, and they were colluding with people nearby. Everyone had to sign contracts. In effect, guardianship was temporarily handed over to the camp, which gave it enormous power to control us; even calling the police could get you dragged back, because a loophole in the law was being exploited through the guardians.

People were ranked into three classes that one could move through: the Reform Class, the Conduct-and-Knowledge Class, and the Joy-of-Learning Class. New arrivals were all placed in the Reform Class. In the dorms, the boys were divided into five classes and the girls into two. Class One was full of the fiercest people, Class Two was slightly less so, Class Three had a mix of everyone, Class Four was for younger kids, and Class Five was for the so-called old, weak, sick, and disabled. From Monday to Friday there were three classes in the morning, two in the afternoon, and extra drills at night. People in the Reform Class only got four or five classroom periods a week; the rest of the time was outdoor militarized training, mixed with standing at attention, morning and afternoon runs, and extra night drills depending on the instructors' moods. After about a month we could test into the Conduct-and-Knowledge Class by memorizing five "gratitude" essays, learning a sign-language dance, and answering questions about the ideological material taught in class. Advancement brought more class time and somewhat less training.

There was a point system. Individuals gained points by answering questions in class or helping instructors and teachers, and lost points for sleeping, talking, fighting, or causing trouble. Whole classes also lost points for poor cleaning, noise at night, and similar issues. Scores were settled weekly. If you earned enough points you could get red tickets to buy snacks, cola, or even luosifen. If points were deducted, the supervising instructor's pay could also be docked, so the instructor would pressure the class monitor, and that person would often be subjected to physical training punishment or direct corporal punishment. Under that pressure, almost nobody could refuse punishment.

There were several psychologists, each responsible for different groups. Some were kinder and spoke soothingly; others were the opposite. Every week we could be called in for about an hour of counseling. At first it was one-on-one, but later it was often done in groups and resembled the routines of a psychiatric hospital. Performance was everything: if you did not "perform" well, you were not getting out. The more you gave up, the harder it was to leave, so people were forced to struggle half-dead just to get out sooner.

If you wanted to talk to your parents, you first had to write a letter. Paper and pens were treated as important supplies. In the Reform Class each person was given two notebooks and one pen, one notebook for weekly summaries and one for reflections, and after class they were collected back into storage; you could not take extra notebooks or pens to the dorm. Once you reached the Conduct-and-Knowledge Class or above, notebooks and pens became more freely available. After about two months you could call home, but only if the supervising instructor signed off on the phone slip, and the psychologist also had to arrange it. Instructors could refuse based on your so-called performance.

On weekends they often played films in the classroom, and if it rained we rested in the dorms, chatted, read, or wrote. The dorms had beds, storage boxes, washstands, and urine buckets, and students kept most of their belongings either in the boxes or hidden under mattresses and quilts because instructors rarely checked carefully. The camp itself was small. It had once been part of another camp and was later rebuilt after that place shut down. It had two-story dormitories, six standard classrooms, a rooftop exercise yard, a basketball court, communal toilets that also served as showers, and long sinks in the corridor for washing clothes. Girls were managed by one female instructor and three female life staff, while the male instructors generally interfered less in the girls' area.

Advancing to the Joy-of-Learning Class was extremely difficult. Out of roughly 180 to 200 people in the camp, only about 9 to 16 were in that class. After two months you could test for it, but the requirements were much harder, including mastering more content and several sign-language dances. That class had the easiest life, with most of the week spent in self-study in the classroom apart from the usual runs and standing at attention. Sometimes students in that class could even join outside activities marketed as study tours. The food standards were actually not low by camp standards: lunch and dinner usually included meat, three dishes, and rice, and there were nightly snacks if you ate quickly enough.

Because of an incident in 2024, the camp later started controlling fights more strictly. Ordinary physical training became somewhat lighter than before. Overall, you could survive there, and compared with some even more brutal camps it may have seemed like the "brightest" one, but it was still a camp. It now tries to look polished and claim to be free of corporal punishment, but the ideological imprisonment at the core of the system is still there.
data.scandal:
According to second-hand accounts from former students, corporal punishment before 2024 was extremely severe, with frequent fights, group beatings, and abuse. Blind spots in the dormitory area and old black rooms were allegedly used for assaults, and in the shower people were reportedly blinded with body wash, the lights were turned off, and then they were beaten. Infectious disease was also said to spread widely with little intervention.

In 2024, eight people reportedly organized an escape. After they were caught and the police only made a token response, those who remained were allegedly subjected to further abuse, and only then did the camp begin controlling fights more strictly. The institution is also accused of deceiving both students and parents.

The rest of this entry consists of additional former-student testimony describing extreme running punishments, beatings with batons, being forced to smell waste or drink contaminated water, injuries such as broken ribs, and other forms of abuse. The account argues that the institution now tries to market itself as free of corporal punishment while the coercive system remains unchanged.
